message.reasoning_content:
We need to fill the skeleton for paper 2011.10206. The extraction task: identify all candidates the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way. The paper presents 591 "high velocity star candidates" (HiVelSCs), selected from LAMOST DR7 and Gaia DR2. The paper states that 43 have unbound probability >50% in Watkins+2019 potential, and up to 304 in Xue+2008. It also classifies into HVS, HRS, RS, OUT. We need to include all 591 candidates that the paper treats as possibly unbound? The extraction skill says: "Include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way / Galactic potential." The paper's selection: those that can escape in at least one of seven potential models, or have total velocities in the Galactic rest frame larger than 450 km/s. They ended up with 591. They also discuss that 43 are unbound in Watkins+2019 with Pub > 50%, and up to 304 in Xue+2008. So all 591 are "high velocity star candidates" but not all are necessarily possibly unbound? The paper's abstract: "We show that at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound to the Galaxy with escape probabilities larger than 50%". Then they mention "a catalog for these 591 HiVelSCs". The selection criteria: "can escape from the Galaxy under at least one potential model or bound but at least with total velocities ... >= 450 km/s". So many are bound but with high velocities. The skill says: "Exclude ordinary runaways, cluster escapers, locally unbound Galactic-center stars that the paper says remain Galaxy-bound, high-velocity halo stars, and objects the paper concludes are bound to the Galaxy." Here, many of the 591 are bound under all potential models (e.g., 287 are bound in Xue+2008). The paper does not conclude that all are unbound; it's a catalog of high-velocity stars, some possibly unbound. We need to determine which ones the paper considers as "possibly unbound". The paper says: "31,440 samples are picked out with the condition of either escaping from our Galaxy under at least one potential model or bound but at least with total velocities of V_GC >= 450 km s^{-1} as used in Marchetti et al. (2019) to select HivelSCs." Then after further filtering, they got 591. The definition of HiVelSCs is broad. The paper does not assert that all 591 are possibly unbound; rather, they are candidates selected based on high velocity, some of which are possibly unbound. The skill's inclusion criteria: "Include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way". The paper explicitly identifies the number of unbound candidates under each potential, and the classification (HVS, HRS, RS, OUT) which is about origins, not bound/unbound. The paper discusses how many are unbound under each model. It does not treat all 591 as possibly unbound; only those with Pub > 50% in some model? The phrase "possibly unbound" appears in the sense that some stars might be unbound. The paper's selection included stars that may escape under at least one potential model, i.e., they are considered as having at least some chance of being unbound. So all 591 are "candidates" that might be unbound, but the paper does not say they are all possibly unbound; they were selected as high velocity, and then some turned out to be possibly unbound. The skill's instruction: "Do not infer a candidate only because a table/caption/catalog review mentions 'high velocity', 'candidate', or a velocity above a generic cutoff." And "Exclude ... objects the paper concludes are bound to the Galaxy." So we must only include those that the paper states might be unbound. The paper says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound to the Galaxy with escape probabilities larger than 50%". It also says "whether the remaining 261 stars are marginally unbound with Pub > 50% ... depends on the potential model used." That suggests that the paper treats those 261 as possibly unbound depending on model. But we need to be exhaustive: include all objects that the paper treats as possibly unbound. The paper's Table tab:punbound_50 shows numbers unbound under each model. The full catalog of 591 includes 43, 52, 59, 97, 149, 211, 304 unbound under respective models. The 287 bound in Xue+2008 are clearly bound under that model, but they might be unbound under other models? Actually, the table indicates that 304 are unbound in Xue+2008 with Pub>=50%, so 287 are bound in Xue+2008 with Pub<50%. Those 287 might still be unbound under other models? Not necessarily: if a star is bound in Xue+2008 (the least conservative), it's bound in all? The paper states: "43 HiVelSCs have Pub>50% of being unbound in Watkins+2019 potential model, and at most 304 HiVelSCs have Pub>50% of being unbound in Xue+2008 potential model". So the 304 includes the 43 plus others. So 304 are possibly unbound in some model? Actually, all 304 are unbound in Xue+2008, which the paper says results in smallest escaping velocities, so those are unbound under that model. So at least 304 are "possibly unbound" under Xue+2008. The rest (287) are bound even under Xue+2008, so they are not even possibly unbound under any model? The paper might still consider them as high velocity stars but bound. So we need to decide which candidates to include. The skill: "Include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way / Galactic potential." The paper explicitly says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound to the Galaxy ... and this number decreases to eight if the possible parallax zero-point error is corrected." It also says "The number of unbound HiVelSCs in Watkins+2019 potential model with Pub>=50% decreased from 43 to 8". So the paper is treating unbound status as model dependent. The paper's selection included stars that might be unbound, but then they calculate unbound probabilities. The question: does the paper treat all 591 as candidate high-velocity stars that could be unbound? The abstract says "we report 591 high velocity star candidates (HiVelSCs) selected from ... with three-dimensional velocities in the Galactic rest-frame larger than 445 km/s." and "We show that at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound to the Galaxy with escape probabilities larger than 50%". The term "candidates" in the paper refers to the 591 selected HiVelSCs, not specifically unbound candidates. The paper's title is "591 high velocity stars in the Galactic halo ...". So they are high velocity star candidates, not necessarily unbound candidates. The paper does not treat all 591 as possibly unbound. It calculates unbound probabilities and finds some unbound. So we should only include those with Pub>50% in at least one of the seven potential models? That would be the ones the paper says are possibly unbound. Which ones exactly? The paper's Table punbound_50 gives counts per model, but not the individual IDs (only the 20 fastest are shown individually). The full catalog (external CSV) has Pub values for each candidate under each model. We only have ECSV tables for the 20 fastest. We don't have the full 591 data, so we can't extract the full list. However, the extraction skill says to be exhaustive. But we only have the LaTeX source, which lists the 20 fastest individually, and summary counts. We don't have the external CSV; the catalog_extraction.json lists the external URL but we cannot access it. The skill says "Before filling the file... you are allowed to use includes verbatim in the user message; you cannot open files or browse. Text and table files are line-numbered." So we only have what's in the user message: the catalog_review.json, catalog_extraction.json, and the ECSV files for tables 1-9, and the LaTeX source. The external CSV is not included. So we cannot extract the full 591 candidates. However, the paper text does not provide a list of all 591. The only individual data are for the 20 fastest (Hivel1–Hivel20). The paper states that "only 14 of them were reported in the current version of the open fast stars catalog". And the 20 fastest are explicitly listed. For the other candidates, no identifiers or values are provided in the paper. The paper catalogs the 591 externally. So we can only include the 20 fastest which we have data for. But the skill's requirement: "The candidates list must be exhaustive: include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way, even when there are dozens; never truncate, sample, or pick representatives." However, we are limited to the data available in the provided files. The user gave us the skeleton and all input files (including the LaTeX source and ECSV tables). The ECSV tables include only the 20 fastest (and summary tables). So the full 591 are not extractable from the provided materials. But the skill says "Use paper text and LaTeX source for candidate rationale, method steps, field definitions, and values missing from ECSV." Yet we can't extract identifiers and data for the remaining 571. The extraction status could be "partial" if we cannot access the full catalog. The skill allows status "partial" for cases where not all data are available. The extraction status options: candidates_found, no_candidates, partial, needs_review, source_missing. Since we have only a subset of candidates, we can set status "partial". We can include the 20 fastest as candidates, and note that the remainder are in an external catalog not included. The skill says "Do not infer a candidate only because a table/caption/catalog review mentions 'high velocity', 'candidate', or a velocity above a generic cutoff." But the 20 fastest are explicitly listed in tables that the paper describes as "the 20 fastest HiVelSCs". The paper's Table tab:basic lists them, and Table tab:kinematic gives their velocities. They are clearly high-velocity candidates. But do we know which of them are possibly unbound? The paper says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound ..." and the 20 fastest likely include many that are unbound under some models (Table tab:unbound shows their Pub values). From Table tab:unbound, Hivel1 has Pub(W)=0.996, etc. So many have Pub>50% under various models. So we can include them. But what about the other candidates that are not in the 20? We don't have data, so we can't include them. The comprehensive list would require access to the external CSV, which is not available. So we set status=partial and explain in summary that only the 20 fastest are included, the full 591 catalog is externally hosted and not available. But the skill says to be exhaustive and never truncate. This is contradictory. I think the instruction is to include every candidate that the paper treats as possibly unbound, given the available data. Since only 20 are extractable, we can include those 20 and note the limitation. The skeleton originally had "needs_review". We'll change to "partial". We'll include 20 candidates, and for candidate_groups_considered, we'll note the full 591 HiVelSCs sample, but only 20 have individual data in the paper. The paper does not provide individual data for the rest, only summary counts and classification. So we cannot extract them individually. So we'll set extraction.status="partial". We'll still create a complete method_chain, but only include the 20 candidates. We'll indicate in summary that the full 591 candidates are in external catalog (resource-001) and that only the 20 fastest are detailed in the paper.

We need to determine what the paper claims for each candidate in terms of unbound status. For the 20 fastest, we have Pub values under seven models. The paper does not state a single bound/unbound claim for each candidate, but rather the unbound probability. We can set galactic_bound_claim to "possibly_unbound" for those with Pub>50% in at least one model, and for others? For the 20 fastest, all have Pub(W) > 0.5? Check table-007: Hivel8: 0.992, Hivel10: 0.837, etc. All 20 have Pub(W) >= 0.659? Hivel17: 0.659. So all are unbound in Watkins+2019 with Pub>=50%? Actually 0.659 > 0.5, yes. So all 20 are unbound in Watkins+2019? Hivel17: Pub(W)=0.659. So all 20 have Pub>0.5 in at least one model (Watkins+2019). So they are all possibly unbound under that model. So we can set galactic_bound_claim: "possibly_unbound" for all, or "unbound" in some? The paper defines unbound if Pub>50%. The paper says "43 HiVelSCs are unbound ... with escape probabilities larger than 50% in Watkins+2019". So these 20 fastest are among them. So we can claim "unbound" under that model? But the paper acknowledges model dependence. The paper's language: "We show that at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound to the Galaxy with escape probabilities larger than 50%". That suggests they consider those 43 as "unbound". So for those, galactic_bound_claim should be "unbound". However, later they note that with zero-point correction, number drops to 8. So it's not a definitive bound/unbound claim. The schema's galactic_bound_claim enum includes "unbound", "likely_unbound", "possibly_unbound", "escaping", "not_reported". We can use "likely_unbound" for those with Pub>50% in Watkins+2019, which the paper treats as the most conservative. For these 20, Pub(W)>=0.5, so they are among the 43. So we can set galactic_bound_claim: "likely_unbound" (since it's probabilistic). The paper says "unbound" for those with Pub>50%, so I'll use "unbound". But note that the paper says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound", so they treat Pub>50% as "unbound". So I'll set "unbound" for all 20. However, we need to be consistent. The paper also mentions that with more conservative criteria, 92 conservative HiVelSCs have 0 unbound in Watkins+2019; but our candidates are not necessarily conservative. So we can go with "unbound". The schema enum: unbound, likely_unbound, possibly_unbound, escaping, not_reported. "unbound" fits.

Now, method_chain: we need to build a DAG. Paper steps:
- Input catalog: LAMOST DR7 (spectra, RV, atmospheric params) and Gaia DR2 (astrometry, photometry).
- Cross-match: LAMOST DR7 with Gaia DR2 using TOPCAT, radius 5 arcsec.
- Quality cuts: stars with positive parallax, f_varpi <= 0.2, and later RV reliability via visual inspection of spectra; only spectra with highest S/N kept; RV from LASP used; then zero-point correction.
- Distance estimation: invert parallax (heliocentric distance). But they use MC and median distance? Actually, they use MC sampling with multivariate Gaussian to get Galactocentric distance and velocity, taking into account correlations. They also consider solar position and motion assumptions.
- Radial velocity measurement: from LAMOST LASP, zero-point corrected.
- Solar position and motion: R0=8.2 kpc, z0=25 pc, v_circ=238 km/s, solar peculiar motion [14.0, 12.24, 7.25] km/s. Steps for velocity calculation and Galactic potential.
- Velocity calculation: Galactocentric Cartesian coordinates and velocities using MC. 
- Galactic potential models: seven models used to estimate escape velocities and unbound probabilities.
- Escape/bound assessment: Pub defined as percentage of MC realizations where V_GC > V_esc. They count number with Pub>50%.
- Orbit integration: with Galpy using MWPotential2014 to get orbit parameters, minimum crossing radius, etc., and classification into HVS, HRS, RS, OUT based on Pgc, PMW, Pub. Also, they derive origin probabilities. So we can split into steps.

We'll create method steps: 
step-01: input_catalog (LAMOST DR7, Gaia DR2)
step-02: cross_match (radius 5 arcsec)
step-03: sample_selection (positive parallax, f_varpi <= 0.2, keep highest S/N per star, visual check of spectra for reliable RV, selection of potentially unbound or high-velocity: either escape in at least one potential model or V_GC >= 450 km/s; then select 591 with reliable RV_LASP after zero-point correction)
step-04: radial_velocity_measurement (LAMOST LASP RV with zero-point correction)
step-05: astrometric_calibration (Gaia DR2 astrometry with correlation matrix; also quality flag for astrometric parameters)
step-06: distance_estimation (MC sampling, using parallax inversion, but they compute Galactocentric distance via coordinates with distance derived from 1/parallax? Actually they say "Using the five astrometric parameters ... Galactocentric distance and total velocity are computed based on ...". They use MC to get r_GC and V_GC. So distance estimation is part of the MC pipeline that also uses solar position and velocity. We could separate solar_position_and_motion step first.
step-07: solar_position_and_motion (R0, z0, v_circ, solar peculiar)
step-08: velocity_calculation (compute Galactocentric x,y,z, Vx, Vy, Vz, V_GC via MC, using above assumptions)
step-09: galactic_potential_model (seven models, compute escape velocity at each star's position)
step-10: escape_or_bound_assessment (Pub calculation)
step-11: orbit_integration (with Galpy MWPotential2014, trace back, get orbit parameters, r_min, Pgc, PMW)
step-12: origin_assessment (classification into HVS, HRS, RS, OUT using criteria in Table origin)

We need to ensure dependencies: step-03 depends on step-01, step-02. step-04, step-05 provide input to velocity/distance. step-07 is needed for step-08. step-08 for step-09? Actually, escape velocity depends only on position (Galactocentric distance), not on velocity. So step-08 may not be needed for escape velocity directly, but they use the MC to get r_GC distribution for each star to compute escape velocities. They likely compute escape velocity at the MC sampled r_GC positions and then compare with V_GC. So step-09 could depend on step-06 (distance) and step-07 (solar position to derive r_GC). So we can set step-09 depends on step-06, step-07. step-08 for V_GC then used in Pub. step-10 depends on step-08, step-09. step-11 orbit uses MWPotential2014, depends on step-07 and step-06, step-05, step-04, step-08? Actually orbit integration needs full phase space, so depends on step-08 (velocity) and step-06 (position) and step-07 (potential). So step-11 depends on step-08, step-07. step-12 depends on step-11 and step-10. We'll include.

Now, candidate_origin: for each candidate, we need to determine if introduced_by_this_paper or cited_from_literature. The paper says only 14 of the 591 were reported in OFSC. The 20 fastest: we need to see which ones were previously known. The paper says "only 14 of them were reported in the current version of the open fast stars catalog", but that's for all 591. Among the 20, possibly some are among those 14? The paper says "only 14 of them were reported in the current version of the open fast stars catalog". We don't know which 14. The paper then says "we cross-match 591 HiVelSCs with Du1 and Du2 ... 12 out of our 591 HiVelSCs were already reported in Du2." So 12 were in Du2. We don't have mapping to the 20 fastest. For the 20 fastest, we need to determine if they are introduced by this paper or cited. Since we can't verify from the text, we might set origin_type "introduced_by_this_paper" unless we can find specific references. The paper does not say which of the 20 are new. It only mentions that 14 were in OFSC, and 12 in Du2. Without explicit IDs, we might need to infer. But the skill says: "introduced_by_this_paper only when this paper first proposes the object as a Galactic-unbound/HVS candidate." But the paper might be the first to propose them as HiVelSCs? Many could be discovered in this search. However, we cannot be sure. Since we have the whole LaTeX, we could check if any of the Hivel IDs are mentioned in relation to previous works. The paper says "only 14 of them were reported in the current version of the open fast stars catalog (OFSC)". It does not list them. So we cannot determine which. We might set origin_type "introduced_by_this_paper" for all 20, since we have no evidence they were cited from literature. But that might be incorrect if some were known. The paper is presenting a new catalog; they claim that only 14 out of 591 were in OFSC. So it's plausible that among the 20 fastest, some are among those 14, but we don't know. The safest is to set "cited_from_literature" only if we can pinpoint. For all 20, we lack references. So we'll set "introduced_by_this_paper" for all, with a note in summary that for the 20 fastest, no prior references found, but some may have been previously known. We can set paper_reassesses_unbound_status: false since they just compute probabilities. But they might reassess? The paper doesn't mention reassessing previously known ones, but they cross-match with OFSC and note only 14 are in it, meaning most are new. For the 20, if they were in OFSC, the paper might mention? The abstract says "only 14 of them were reported in the current version of the open fast stars catalog". So those 14 are among the 591. We cannot determine which. So we'll set origin_type: "introduced_by_this_paper" for each candidate, and add a note in extraction summary that 14 of the 591 were previously known, but without individual identifiers, we treat all 20 as new. We'll also add candidate_origin.source_refs pointing to the paper's statement that only 14 were in OFSC.

Now, we need to fill all the candidate fields. We have ECSV data for the 20 fastest: table-001 (basic), table-002 (kinematic), table-006 (escape velocities), table-007 (unbound probabilities), table-008 (atmospheric params). We need to map columns correctly.

We'll use identifiers: record_id: "2011.10206:cand-001", etc. paper_candidate_id: "Hivel1", "Hivel2", etc. gaia_source_id: not provided in these tables; the catalog includes Gaia_designation but not for 20 fastest, so empty. all[]: can include "Hivel1" and maybe LAMOST specid if given? Not in these tables. So just Hivel ID.

For observed_phase_space: RA, Dec from table-001. They are in decimal degrees. The table header says "R.A. (deg)", "decl. (deg)". The Gaia DR2 coordinates are ICRS at epoch J2015.5 (reference epoch) as per coordinate frame reference. So we set coordinate_format: decimal_degrees, unit: deg, reference_frame: value "ICRS", epoch: value "J2015.5", epoch_kind "reference_epoch", with survey_reference entry_id "gaia-dr2". But the paper may state frame? They just say from Gaia DR2. So we use that. We'll cite source refs to the table cells. Parallax, proper motions, radial velocity from table-001. They are gaia parallax and proper motions, and LASP RV (zero-point corrected). We'll note that the RV column is RV_LASP with zero-point correction, taken from ECSV. The header says RV_LASP^c, and footnote c says "RV given by the LASP, which is corrected a radial velocity zero-point of -5.4 km s^{-1}". So we'll set radial_velocity with raw_value "179 +/- 5" (from table), and we can put measured values accordingly. For distance: not directly in table-001; we have parallax. The derived kinematics table gives Galactocentric positions and velocities, not heliocentric distance. The paper likely does not report observed heliocentric distance directly, but we have parallax. So observed phase space can have parallax but not distance. We'll set distance null. We'll include parallax.

For derived_kinematics: from table-002: galactocentric x, y, z, r_GC, Vx, Vy, Vz, V_GC. We'll fill those with quantities from table-002. Those are given with median, lower and upper uncertainty (16th, 84th percentiles). We can encode raw_value as e.g. "-5.9 _-0.3^+0.4" from ECSV col_002 for x. We'll parse value as -5.9, lower_error 0.3, upper_error 0.4, unit kpc. We'll store raw_value exactly with the underscore notation.

Bound_assessment: escape velocity from table-006 per model, and unbound probabilities from table-007. We need to capture Pub(W) as unbound_probability for the star. Since the paper uses multiple models, we need to report which model. The unbound_probability field can hold one value, but we have seven. We'll need to report the unbound probability under the most conservative model (Watkins+2019) as the main unbound_probability. But the paper says "at least 43 are unbound" using that model. So we'll use Pub(W) from table-007. We'll also include escape_velocity from Watkins+2019 as escape_velocity. We'll note that the values are specific to Watkins+2019 model. The method chain will reference the potential model. We'll include also escape_velocity_ratio? Not needed. So bound_probability could be 1 - Pub(W)? The paper does not give bound probability directly. We'll leave bound_probability null. We'll include unbound_probability and escape_velocity. We could also include Pub for other models as extra. But we'll stick to core.

Photometry: from table-001: G, G_BP, G_RP magnitudes. We'll add photometry records.

Spectroscopy: spectral type from table-001 Class column (e.g., G7). Also maybe S/N_r (quality flag, not spectroscopy). We'll add spectroscopy with measurement_type: spectral_type, value "G7". Also could add RV measurement? Already in phase space. We'll add spectroscopy record for spectral type with instrument LAMOST.

Stellar_parameters: from table-008: Teff_LASP, logg_LASP, [Fe/H]_LASP, and DD-Payne values. We can include Teff and logg, metallicity from LASP as primary, and DD-Payne as extra. We'll put teff: value 4803, error 19, unit K, raw "4803 +/- 19". log_g: 1.89 +/- 0.03 dex. metallicity: -1.40 +/- 0.02 dex. We can also add abundances for alpha element from DD-Payne. We'll include abundance record for [alpha/Fe] with value 0.28, element "alpha". For DD-Payne parameters, we can optionally add extras or other.

Quality_flags: astrometric_flag (0/1) from table-001. We'll add that.

Orbit: from table-002: eccentricity e, Zmax, r_min, E_Phi_infinity. For eccentricity and Zmax, many entries have "-" meaning not provided (for unbound stars or Zmax>200). Table-002 shows for Hivel1 e is "-" so not provided. So we might leave eccentricity null, etc. We'll include r_min and E_Phi_infinity as available. The footnote says e and Zmax only for bound stars with Zmax<=200 kpc. So we'll not put them if missing.

Astrophysical_origin: origin classification from table-007: Origin_Class e.g., "HRS". Also Pgc, PMW, and Pub values per model. We can include hypothesis_metrics for Pub under different models? The schema allows hypothesis_metrics inside astrophysical_origin. We'll include Pub as unbound probability under each model? The origin classification criteria uses Pub, but we already have Pub in core bound assessment. To avoid duplication, we can put Pgc and PMW as origin probabilities. And classification as origin_classification. The table-007 has Pgc, PMW, and Origin_Class. We'll store those. We can add extra for Pub values of other models.

Now, candidate_origin: we'll set origin_type "introduced_by_this_paper" for each, paper_reassesses_unbound_status false. Provide source_refs pointing to the text that says only 14 were in OFSC (line 302). That implies the rest are new. For the 20, we can't confirm, but we'll note that.

Now, inclusion_assessment: we'll set paper_labels: ["hvs_candidate"]? Actually the paper uses "HiVelSCs" (high velocity star candidates) which includes HVS, HRS, RS, OUT. So they are not necessarily all HVS; they are "high velocity star candidates". The schema enum includes "hvs_candidate", "hyper_runaway_candidate", "escaping_star", "unbound_star", "high_velocity_star", "runaway_candidate", "candidate_group_member", "other". The paper explicitly calls them "high velocity star candidates (HiVelSCs)". So we can use "high_velocity_star". The paper also classifies them into HVS, HRS, RS, OUT. For a given star, its classification might be "HRS" etc. We could use "hyper_runaway_candidate" for HRS, "runaway_candidate" for RS, "hvs_candidate" for HVS, and "other" for OUT? The OUT are fast halo stars not considered as any of those. The schema paper_labels can be multiple. We'll set paper_labels based on the classification from table-007 Origin_Class. For Hivel1 is HRS, so "hyper_runaway_candidate". For Hivel4 is OUT, so "other" and maybe "fast_halo_star"? The enum has "other" so we'll put "other" for OUT. We'll also add "high_velocity_star" for all.

galactic_bound_claim: We'll set "unbound" as discussed.

inclusion_basis: the paper explicitly says 43 are unbound with Pub>50%, and the 20 fastest are among them, so we can use "explicit_unbound_text" and "candidate_table_with_text_anchor". The table lists Pub values. The inclusion basis can be "explicit_unbound_text" because the paper explicitly says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound". However, the paper does not list individual IDs for the 43. But the table of unbound probabilities shows all 20 have high Pub, so they are unbound. We'll set inclusion_basis "explicit_unbound_text" and "candidate_table_with_text_anchor". We'll cite the text about 43 unbound and the table.

Extraction confidence: high, since all data present.

source_refs: we'll cite relevant lines.

Now, we need to produce the full JSON. We'll update extraction status to "partial", summary describing missing full catalog. tooling: we'll leave the placeholders as they are (the skeleton had empty tooling). The skill says "Fill extraction.tooling with the real instrument provenance ... batch pipelines fill them programmatically from the API request/response." Since we are agent, we'll leave the placeholders empty. But the validator `--require-complete` rejects empty model_id/prompt_version. The skeleton had empty strings. We need to not trigger validation in this step? The user just wants to complete the JSON. We'll leave tooling as empty strings, but validation later might fail. It's not our problem. The user may run validation separately. We'll fill extraction as required: status "partial", summary "Extraction of the 20 fastest HiVelSCs from the paper; the full 591 candidate catalog (external CSV) not available. Only 20 candidates included.", etc. We'll set extracted_at to current time, extractor "agent".

Now, we need to structure candidate_groups_considered. Since we are including the 20 fastest, we need to mention that the full 591 sample was considered but not individually extractable. We'll add candidat_groups_considered for the full sample with decision "partial_inclusion", reason "full catalog of 591 HiVelSCs is available externally; only 20 are detailed in the paper, so extraction is limited to those 20.".

Now, we'll build JSON.

Let's begin building the result.
